coredata.dc:title: Effect of surfactant surface nature on the energy transfer efficiency (η) of a carbon dot-dye system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Jana, Jayasmita
Yoo, Ik Keun
Chung, Jin Suk
Hur, Seung Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have unique optoelectronic properties and are considered efficient fluorescent probes as organic dyes, semiconducting quantum dots, metallic clusters, etc. A carbon dot-dye system has been established to study alternative photoelectric conversion processes. For this purpose, a CuO-associated CD was synthesized using a green chemical method. The energy conversion efficiency was monitored using a cationic, anionic, and neutral dye. The nature of the dye has an important effect on the energy transfer process. This limitation from its electrostatic nature was overcome by introducing a surfactant into the system. Both positively and negatively charged surfactants were used in this study. The nature of the surfactant was found to have a significant effect on the energy transfer process within positively charged CDs and organic dyes. The individual interactions within surfactant-CD and surfactant-dye system can cause modification. This CD-dye-surfactant system with further modifications can be used appropriately in dye-sensitized solar cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Organic dye
Surfactant
Quenching efficiency
Energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interaction between CDC and organic dyes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDC (inset: particle size distribution). (B) XRD pattern of CDC (inset: SAED pattern of CDC).
(A) Broad range XPS spectrum of CDC. Elemental analysis of CDC (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) Cu2p. (E) FTIR spectrum of CDC.
(A) Spectral profile of CDC. (B) Fluorescence decay profile of CDC. λex = 360 nm.
Fluorescence of CDC in the presence of different dye concentrations. (A) FlC, (B) ArS and (C) RhB. (D) Fluorescence decay profile of CDC in the presence of RhB. λex = 360 nm.
RhB concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDC in (A) CTAB and (B) SDS medium. λex = 360 nm.
RhB concentration-dependent fluorescence decay profile of CDC in (A) CTAB and (B) SDS medium. λex = 360 nm.
RhB concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDC in (A) DTMA and (B) DCS medium. λex = 360 nm.

J-V characteristics of RhB-sensitized ZnO solar cells containing CDC (blue line), CDC-CTAB (black line) and CDC-SDS (red line) under (100 mWcm−2) illumination. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Effect of surfactant surface nature on the energy transfer efficiency (η) of a carbon dot-dye system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have unique optoelectronic properties and are considered efficient fluorescent probes as organic dyes, semiconducting quantum dots, metallic clusters, etc. A carbon dot-dye system has been established to study alternative photoelectric conversion processes. For this purpose, a CuO-associated CD was synthesized using a green chemical method. The energy conversion efficiency was monitored using a cationic, anionic, and neutral dye. The nature of the dye has an important effect on the energy transfer process. This limitation from its electrostatic nature was overcome by introducing a surfactant into the system. Both positively and negatively charged surfactants were used in this study. The nature of the surfactant was found to have a significant effect on the energy transfer process within positively charged CDs and organic dyes. The individual interactions within surfactant-CD and surfactant-dye system can cause modification. This CD-dye-surfactant system with further modifications can be used appropriately in dye-sensitized solar cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy has gained importance because of its sensitivity with a high signal to noise ratio, simple operation, sensitivity, and ability to measure multiple emissive properties [1]. Among the different processes reported thus far, the Fӧrster resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomenon has attracted considerable attention because of its application to a wide range of research of immunoassays, chemicals, and bio-sensing [2,3]. In FRET, fluctuations of the detection of analytes is reduced because it considers the fluorescence intensity ratio of donor and acceptor moieties. FRET phenomenon depends on factors, such as the distance between donor and acceptor (~80 Å), spectral overlap of donor emission and acceptor absorption, and the orientation of the chromophore [4]. Often the Poisson statistics [5] is used to determine the donor/acceptor ratio. If the Poisson distribution fails, the FRET exponent becomes lower and distance dependence looks much weaker than it really is. In the FRET phenomenon, the energy transfer efficiency depends on the emission properties of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor [6]. Moroz et al. [7]. Baene et al. [5], and Das et al. [8] reported the dependence of FRET on different parameters. Dworak et al. [9] showed an acceptor concentration FRET system in the perylene diimide−quantum dot organic−inorganic hybrid system. The choice of donor-acceptor molecule involved is also important. Fӧrster initially considered a conjugated fluorescent molecule of low molecular weight [5]. Recently, studies on high molecular weight polymeric fluorescent molecules, quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots, etc. have shown significant results.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are promising alternatives for conventional semiconductors, quantum dots, and organic dyes [10]. CDs are widely applicable in the fields of sensing [11], imaging [12], drug delivery [13], photovoltaics [14], and catalysis [15] because of their broad absorption range, tunable photoluminescence, water solubility, photo-resistance, and low cytotoxicity properties [16]. The physical properties can be changed by tuning the size, synthetic process, medium, etc. [12,17]. The unique optoelectronic properties of CDs make it more suitable for donors [18] in FRET systems than other materials. The broad absorption, tunable emission, long lifetime, and high quantum yield are the properties that ensure the eligibility of CDs as a donor in a FRET system. Previously, Pons et al. [19] considered a QD-dye system to establish a FRET interaction within the system considering a QD as a point dipole. The use of a dye molecule as an acceptor throws the challenge of the breakdown of a point dipole approximation depending on the proximity with the donor surface [5]. Blaudeck et al. reported that electron tunneling from QDs to the environment, for 5 Å for a 3.5-nm-diameter CdSe QD, led to additional luminescence quenching in terms of electron transfer within the FRET system. Because CDs also have certain optoelectronic properties comparable to QDs, a study of CDs is expected to produce new dimensions towards FRET studies. Organic dyes are well known fluorescent probes that are successfully applied in biological and energy fields [20–25]. The electronic and molecular properties of organic dyes initiated the study on CD/QD-dye conjugate system [26,27]. The broad excitation spectra and large absorption cross section of CD and narrow excitation spectra of dye makes them a good candidate for FRET pair [28].

In the present work, CuO-associated nitrogen doped carbon dots (CDCs) were synthesized using a microwave synthesis method. CDs possess unsaturated carbon skeleton and abundant functional groups on the surface, and they can be compared with pigments or polycyclic aromatic compounds. The metal oxide support and hetero atom dopant was provided to enhance the electron transfer ability of the CDC particles. The electron transfer efficiency was examined through the FRET process. For this purpose, organic dyes (cationic, anionic, and neutral) have been utilized as acceptors along with CDC donor. The energy conversion efficiency (η) depends strongly on the electrostatic nature of the dyes. The as-synthesized CDC particles possess a zeta potential and show high-energy transfer efficiency with negative and neutral dyes. For cationic xanthene dyes, such as Rhodamine B, the efficiency is not significant, even though the dye-CDC system showed a significant increase in absorbance. To overcome this limitation, few cationic and anionic surfactants were introduced to the dye-CDC system. In addition, the nature of the surfactant has a range of effects on the system. The experimental data have been explained adequately.

Detailed descriptions of the materials and instrumental characterization are shown in the supporting information. To synthesize the CDCs, a one-step microwave-assisted carbonization method was adopted. Typically, 0.1 M 1 mL citric acid (CA), 0.1 M 0.5 mL tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA), and 0.01 M 0.5 mL copper sulfate pentahydrate [CuSO4,5H2O] were added sequentially to a 40 mL glass beaker. The mixture was sonicated to form a homogeneous solution and heated in a domestic microwave for 5 min. During this process, a brown colored solid was obtained. After cooling, the resulting solid was dissolved in water. The resulting solution was subjected to centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) to remove large agglomerated particles. The CDC supernatant was then purified by dialysis [molecular cut-off 100–500 kDa] for 12 h. Finally, a clear pale-yellow aqueous dispersion containing the CDCs was obtained.

CDC showed an intense emission at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 3 ). From the fluorescence decay profile, the average lifetime was calculated to be 12.92 ns (Fig. 3). The suitable excitation wavelength was chosen using a trial and error method (Fig. S1). The absorption and excitation spectra also confirmed the excitation wavelength selected. The absorption spectra showed peaks at 260 nm and 358 nm, which were assigned to n-π* and π-π* transitions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between CDC and organic dyes
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are often compared to traditional organic dyes in terms of intense fluorescence. On the other hand, CDs with significant non-toxicity, resistance towards photobleaching, broad absorbance region, tunable emission, etc. have gained considerable importance over dyes. Traditional organic dyes have a very narrow excitation range [38] and weak photostability. The wide range of optical properties over the visible region to the near infrared region compensates for the shortcomings of traditional organic dyes in the near-infrared region.

Because CDs have the ability of a facile electronic interaction with a suitable acceptor, they can be used successfully for the construction of a FRET system, bio-mimicking photoelectronic devices, etc. [12] Different types of organic dyes are used to construct a photoelectron transfer system. For this purpose, a cationic dye, Rhodamine B (RhB), an anionic dye, alizarin red S (ArS), and a neutral dye, fluorescein C (FlC), were used. Different spectroscopic studies have been performed to calculate the energy transfer efficiency.

FlC is a fluorescent dye that contains = O, –OH, and –COOH groups at the surface. This dye interacts with CDC strongly, resulting in the quenching of CDC fluorescence over the FlC concentration range of 0–0.105 mM. At 0.105 mM FlC, the η value for 0.01 g/mL CDC was calculated to be 78.4% using the following equation:
(1)
η =
Ι
D A
Ι D
where IDA and ID denoted the intensities of the donor in the presence and absence of acceptor molecules.

This CDC fluorescence quenching is accompanied by the enhancement of an FlC fluorescence intensity at 360 nm excitation (Fig. 4 A). This quenching in CDC fluorescence is associated with a shift of the emission peak towards the blue region, indicating some electron transfer involving surface functional groups. The quenching of CDC fluorescence was also observed in the presence of ArS. The η value was calculated to be 70.7% for 0.01 g/mL CDC at 0.105 mM concentration of ArS (Fig. 4B). ArS in a weakly fluorescent dye with –SO3 -, =O, and –OH functionalities. CDC showed stronger fluorescence than ArS; the fluorescent peak of ArS could not be observed, even with increasing ArS concentration. The strong overlap between CDC emission and dye absorption peak (Fig. S2) suggests significant energy transfer within the CDC-dye system. The same type of spectral overlap was observed with the RhB-CDC system. The absorption peak of CDC increased in the presence of RhB (Fig. S3). The enhanced absorption would contribute to the light-harvesting consequently promoting the photoelectric conversion efficiency [39] to the dye-CDC system. On the other hand, for RhB, the extent of energy transfer was not significant. The calculated η value was 6.03% for 0.01 g/mL CDC at 0.105 mM RhB. The weak interaction might be due to electrostatic repulsion between two positively charged moieties. Both molecules with a bulky structure might face sterically inappropriate positions.

The weak interaction was also reflected in their fluorescence decay profile (Fig. 4C). The average lifetimes were calculated using the following equation via the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method,
(2)
τ
a v
=
∑
n = 1
x
α n
τ n 2
∑
n = 1
x
α n
τ n
where α and τ are the relative % and lifetime values of x number of components of the fluorescent moiety. The quenching efficiency was calculated to be 9.1% using the following equation:
(3)
η
F R E T
=
(
1 −
τ
D A
τ D
)
where ηFRET is the FRET efficiency; τDA and τD denote the lifetime values of the donor in the presence and absence of an acceptor, respectively.

The lifetime of CDC in the absence and presence of 0.105 mM RhB was calculated to be 12.92 ns and 11.76 ns, respectively. The lifetime of the fluorophore denotes the time of stay of the fluorophore at the excited state. The experimental results showed that the FRET system was not ready for further applications. Therefore, further modifications were needed to enhance the FRET efficiency. The FRET efficiency depends on the distance between the donor and acceptor [7], electronic coupling between the donor and acceptor [40], and adjusting the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO-LUMO) gap for the donor to avoid interference from the electrolyte [41]. Therefore, tuning of any these properties would modify the process.

The effects of CTAB and SDS were examined separately on the emission of CDC and RhB. Fig. S5 shows that in the presence of SDS, the emission of CDC increased to some extent, whereas it remained almost unchanged in the presence of CTAB. The positively charged CDC might be adsorbed on SDS, carrying a negative charge, due to electrostatic adsorption. For CTAB, the electrostatic interactions within CTAB and CDC might be unfavorable, but a weak hydrophobic effect on the emission of CDC occurs. For RhB, the emission increased in CTAB but remained relatively unchanged in SDS. In addition, no change in the physical appearance of the solutions was observed, indicating the absence of aggregation or any chemical interaction. The CDC-RhB interaction changed in the presence of CTAB and SDS. Therefore, it was assumed that confinement might be the reason because no other physical or chemical interactions that could explain the improved FRET efficiency were found.

In addition to the change in emission, the absorption spectra also changed. Pure RhB had an absorbance in the range of 450–600 nm, which increased in the presence of CDC. In the surfactant environment, the peak for RhB increased further along with a hyperchromic effect. This enhancement was greater in the SDS medium than in CTAB medium (Fig. S6). This enhancement in absorbance might be due to the electron donating/accepting interaction within CDC and RhB that caused the decrease in molecule coplanarity and disturbance in the conjugation [39]. This suggests that the interaction was more significant in the SDS medium. To confirm this, two other cationic and anionic surfactants, i.e., dodecyltrimethylammonium chloride (DTMA) and docusate sodium (DCS), were introduced to CDC-RhB system individually. Fig. 7 shows that the transfer efficiency increased significantly. The η values in the presence of DTMA and DSC were calculated to be 16% and 23%, respectively. During the study with surfactants, the transfer efficiency was higher in the anionic surfactants than cationic ones. As the size and functionality of the surfactants were not comparable, the efficiencies were not compared with respect to the electrostatic nature of the surfactants. From electrostatic point of view, negatively charged surfactants pull out more efficiency for energy transfer. The effects of surfactants, CTAB and SDS, were checked on FlC and ArS to ensure the impact of the electrostatic nature. The results are shown in Fig. S7. The η values were calculated according to equation (1). For FlC, the η values were calculated to be 74.1% and 78.7%, respectively. These values are closer to the η values observed without surfactants. The η values for the CDC-ArS system in SDS and CTAB systems were calculated to be 65.4% and 69.0%, respectively. These values suggest that in addition to the CDC-surfactant interaction, the dye-surfactant interaction also plays an important role. Under a surfactant environment, the optical properties and nature of ArS were affected [38]. Therefore, the interaction with CDC would also face some modifications. On the other hand, the differently charged organic dyes behaved differently towards CDC under confinement. Thus, the surface natures of as-synthesized CDs are very important to establish any CD-dye interaction for different sensor and device related applications.

The enhanced absorption suggested that the CDC-RhB system might contribute to light harvesting and promote the photoelectric conversion efficiency of the dye-semiconductor complex system for any dye-sensitized solar cell. To examine the energy/electron transfer process, a photochemical experiment was carried out using a CDC-RhB/ZnO system in a two-electrode cell system with a platinum counter electrode. ZnO was prepared by a hydrothermal treatment of zinc acetate under an alkaline medium. Fig. S8 shows that the as-synthesized ZnO nanoparticles are hexagonal in structure. XRD revealed the presence of different planes of ZnO. The electrodes were prepared by CDC-RhB, CDC-RhB-CTAB, and CDC-RhB-SDS systems using a simple electrodeposition method. The current density vs. potential plot indicates that the use of only the CDC-RhB system showed an insignificant current density, while the incorporation of CTAB/SDS produced better results (Fig. 8 ). The power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) was calculated using the following equation [46]:
(4)
η
p C E
=
F F ×
J
s C
×
V
0 C
p
i n
× 100 %
where FF, JSC, VOC, and pin are the filling factor, short circuit current, open circuit voltage, and input power (100 mWcm−2), respectively. In the J-V plot, the current of CDC-RhB-CTAB was higher than that of the CDC-RhB-SDS system. Owing to higher FF and VOC values, however, the ηPCE value was similar, ~0.3 × 10−5, which is several orders of magnitude higher than that of CDC-RhB. It has been reported that CD particles can absorb photon and generate an additional energy level within π of carbon and π* of oxygen [47]. Linking the CD and dye to each other would make the use of wide absorption region of CD and high quantum yield of dye. FRET would leave the dye molecule excited due to absorption of excited photon from excited donor and the shortcoming of narrow absorption could be overcome [48,49]. The dye-CD conjugation would increase the short-circuit current and the open-circuit voltage and thus the conversion efficiency of the DSSC when assembled with a semiconductor and electrolyte [46]. Further experimentation is going on to explore the mechanism and improvement of the current density. In most cases, CD particles are associated with the photoanode to modify the metal oxide energy levels [50], but in this work, the effect of CD particles on dye molecules have been examined.

In this study, it was observed that the nature of as-synthesized CDC played an important role during construction of an energy transfer system with organic dyes. As the CDC with positive surface charge and cationic dye RhB could not show significant energy transfer, the donor-acceptor pair was kept under surfactant confinement for maintaining a suitable interacting distance and thus enhancement of energy transfer. The size and electronic nature of surfactant influenced the FRET system in terms of interaction with RhB and surfactant. Furthermore, the negatively charged surfactants provided better energy transfer efficiency for surface positive CDC. The CDC-RhB-surfactant system was associated to a dye sensitized solar cell to see its practical application of photo-induced energy transfer. Previously, there were reports of RhB based DSSC [51–53], but sometimes co-sensitizer was needed. The additional intermediate for energy/electron transfer within DSSC instead of insulating energy barrier would certainly enhance the efficiency of the dye-semiconductor system. Previous studies showed that CDs could act as one directional intermediate for bridging between dye and the TiO2 in DSSC [27,37]. As FRET within the energy transfer system might be more helpful than co-sensitization approach [54], in the present study the FRET has been utilized to enhance the ηPCE. But, the FRET within RhB and CD might be affected by electrostatic nature, distance between donor-acceptor etc. factors. Our study shows that the FRET can be used for construction of DSSC in association of ZnO with the assistance of both cationic and anionic surfactants. The knowledge of the surface properties of CDs would help to design better photovoltaic devices. Also, appropriate choice of medium could help to achieve better FRET efficiency that in future would help in dye-CD FRET based sensor, diodes, and devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2019R1A2B5B02069683).
